data.body:
## Context
Teams using DSO to plan and execute feature epics face a compounding problem: when integration assumptions embedded in specs are wrong, the failures cascade through implementation, review, and validation — surfacing only after the epic is closed. The v2→v3 ticket system migration demonstrated this at scale: the Jira bridge was designed against ACLI capabilities that didn't exist (no empirical verification during brainstorm), 62+ hotfix commits were needed post-cutover because success criteria were never independently verified at closure, and the adversarial review's taxonomy had no category for consumer impact, so downstream breakage wasn't analyzed. The result was ~48 hours of firefighting and a separate 21-commit cleanup epic. This epic adds defense-in-depth across the planning lifecycle so that these classes of failures are caught during planning (when the user is engaged and can redirect) and verified at closure (before the epic is declared complete). After delivery, teams should experience fewer post-closure integration surprises and be able to confidently declare epics complete without entering multi-day firefighting cycles.

## Success Criteria
1. A dedicated dso:feasibility-reviewer plugin agent is invoked as the 4th brainstorm reviewer when the epic spec contains external integration signals (third-party CLI tools, external APIs/services, CI/CD workflow changes, infrastructure provisioning, data format migrations, authentication/credential flows). The reviewer uses WebSearch to verify external tool capabilities, searches GitHub for known-working integration code, and produces a Technical Feasibility section with verified constraints and identified risks. If feasibility review identifies a critical capability gap (no verified code, no public documentation, no sandbox available), the epic is flagged as high-risk and a spike task is recommended before story acceptance. Epics without external integration signals skip the reviewer.
2. Preplanning includes an integration research step (after story decomposition, before adversarial review). A story undergoes integration research if it references any of: third-party CLI tools, external APIs/services, CI/CD workflow changes, infrastructure provisioning, data format migrations, or authentication/credential flows. For each qualifying story, WebSearch finds known-working code and verifies specific capabilities. Findings are added to the story's Considerations as Verified Integration Constraints. If no sandbox is available for integration testing, this is flagged to the user during preplanning. If research finds no verified code or capabilities, the story is flagged as high-risk and recommended for spike-task creation before implementation.
3. Dedicated dso:red-team-reviewer and dso:blue-team-filter plugin agents replace the current prompted generic agents. The red team agent includes a 6th taxonomy category — Consumer Impact / Operational Readiness — and a Consumer Enumeration directive that identifies all known consumers of the system being modified before analyzing stories. The blue team agent persists the full exchange (accepted findings, rejected findings with rationale) to ARTIFACTS_DIR/adversarial-review-<epic-id>.json, referenced by a one-line ticket comment.
4. The complexity evaluator emits pattern_familiarity (high/medium/low), external_boundary_count (integer), and feasibility_review_recommended (boolean) alongside the existing classification, without changing existing routing thresholds. pattern_familiarity scoring: High = pattern appears in 2+ existing implementations in this repo; Medium = pattern is common in the ecosystem but novel to this repo; Low = novel pattern with no precedent in this repo or ecosystem. Brainstorm uses feasibility_review_recommended to trigger the feasibility reviewer.
5. A dedicated dso:completion-verifier plugin agent independently verifies success criteria (for epics) and done definitions (for stories) before closure. For each criterion/definition, it checks whether the implementation delivers the described outcome — confirming behavioral changes exist, consumers function, and no criteria were skipped or only partially addressed. Test pass/fail, code quality, lint, and formatting are explicitly out of scope (handled by test gate, code review, and hooks respectively). The verifier answers 'did we build what the spec says?' not 'is the code correct?' Failures block closure and create remediation tasks.
6. The completion verifier includes a consumer smoke test: for epics that modify shared infrastructure (ticket system, hooks, merge workflow, sprint tooling), the verifier enumerates known consumers and defines specific verification commands per consumer. These verification commands must pass before epic closure.

## Validation
- Design validation (pre-deployment): Run the feasibility reviewer retroactively against the v2-v3 migration epic specs (w21-24kl, w21-bwfw) to confirm it would have flagged the ACLI capability assumptions. Run the completion verifier against w21-24kl to confirm it would have caught the incomplete v2 reference cleanup and non-functional bridge.
- False-positive calibration: Run the completion verifier against 4-5 past epics spanning different domains (ticket system, skill infrastructure, hooks, scripts) to confirm it correctly distinguishes incomplete from complete.
- Operational before/after: Define integration-related hotfix as: commits that modify tool invocations, CLI flags, API call patterns, workflow triggers, data format handling, or integration sequencing after epic closure. Baseline: 62 in v2-v3 migration. Success target: fewer than 10 combined across the first 2 epics with these gates, no single epic exceeding 6.
- Consumer smoke test effectiveness: For the first 2 epics modifying shared infrastructure, track consumer failures in the 7 days post-closure. Zero = working as designed. Any failures = analyze and adjust coverage.

## Dependencies
None. This epic subsumes the relevant scope of:
- dso-8l5h (Extract review and evaluation sub-agents into dedicated plugin agents) — red team, blue team, feasibility reviewer, and completion verifier extraction
- dso-d63r (Planning Intelligence — Research and Scenario Hardening) — planning research scope

## Approach
Six dedicated plugin agents and skill modifications across brainstorm, preplanning, sprint, and the complexity evaluator. Feasibility review and integration research provide early warning during planning. Expanded adversarial review catches consumer impact gaps. Completion verifier provides independent SC verification at closure. Each layer catches different failure modes — no single gate is relied upon.
